initial.prompt:
<context>
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP).

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell contains a color value (integer 0–9). The goal is to discover the hidden transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.
</context>

<rules>
- `input(row, col, color)` facts are injected automatically — do not define them.
- `color(0..9)` facts are injected automatically — do not define them.
- the output predicate is always `output(row, col, color)` — do not redefine it.
- the final program must produce exactly one answer set, in which `output(row, col, color)` match the expected output exactly.
</rules>

Below are two fully worked examples.

<example_1>
<example_grid_1>

<diagram>

7 7 7
0 7 7
</diagram>

Output:
<diagram>

0 0 0 7 7 7 7 7 7
0 0 0 0 7 7 0 7 7
0 7 7 0 7 7 0 7 7
7 7 7 7 7 7 7 7 7
0 7 7 0 7 7 0 7 7
0 0 0 0 7 7 0 7 7
0 0 0 7 7 7 7 7 7
0 0 0 0 7 7 0 7 7
</diagram>
</example_grid_1>

<transformation>
The input is treated as a tile map. The output is NxM times larger than the input (where the input is N rows x M cols). Each input cell at (BR, BC) with a non-zero value causes a copy of the full input to be placed in the corresponding NxM block of the output (at rows BR*N .. BR*N+N-1, cols BC*M .. BC*M+M-1). Each input cell with value 0 produces an all-zero block at that position.
</transformation>

<asp>
% Count distinct row/col indices — equals grid dimensions for 0-indexed inputs
n_rows(N) :- N = #count{R : input(R, _, _)}.
n_cols(N) :- N = #count{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% Output is NR*NR rows and NC*NC cols (input tiled by itself)
output_cell(R, C) :-
    input(BR, BC, _), input(DR, DC, _),
    n_rows(NR), n_cols(NC),
    R = BR*NR + DR, C = BC*NC + DC.

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% Non-zero block: the sub-cell must equal the input value at the relative position
:- input(BR, BC, V), V != 0,
   input(DR, DC, TileColor),
   n_rows(NR), n_cols(NC),
   R = BR*NR + DR, C = BC*NC + DC,
   output(R, C, ActualColor), ActualColor != TileColor.

% Zero block: every sub-cell must be 0
:- input(BR, BC, 0),
   input(DR, DC, _),
   n_rows(NR), n_cols(NC),
   R = BR*NR + DR, C = BC*NC + DC,
   output(R, C, ActualColor), ActualColor != 0.
</asp>
</example_1>

<transformation>
The output is the same size as the input. Color-3 cells are unchanged. Non-3 cells (value 0) that are enclosed by color-3 walls — i.e., cannot reach the grid boundary via 4-connected movement through non-3 cells — are filled with color 4. All other non-3 cells remain 0.
</transformation>

<asp>
% Output has the same dimensions as the input
output_cell(R, C) :- input(R, C, _).
1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% Grid boundary coordinates
min_row(R) :- R = #min{Row : input(Row, _, _)}.
max_row(R) :- R = #max{Row : input(Row, _, _)}.
min_col(C) :- C = #min{Col : input(_, Col, _)}.
max_col(C) :- C = #max{Col : input(_, Col, _)}.

% 4-connected adjacency offsets
delta(0,1). delta(0,-1). delta(1,0). delta(-1,0).

% Non-3 cells on the grid boundary are reachable from outside
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, min_row(Rmin), R = Rmin.
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, max_row(Rmax), R = Rmax.
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, min_col(Cmin), C = Cmin.
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, max_col(Cmax), C = Cmax.

% Flood-fill outward through non-3 cells
outside(R2, C2) :-
    outside(R1, C1), delta(DR, DC),
    R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
    input(R2, C2, V), V != 3.

% Color-3 cells stay 3
:- input(R, C, 3), output(R, C, Color), Color != 3.

% Outside non-3 cells stay 0
:- outside(R, C), output(R, C, Color), Color != 0.

% Interior non-3 cells (enclosed by 3s, not reachable from boundary) become 4
:- input(R, C, 0), not outside(R, C), output(R, C, Color), Color != 4.
</asp>
</example_2>

full_program:
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI transformation:
%  * Determine the smallest border colour (the “wall”)
%  * Flood‑fill starts at every border cell whose colour ≠ wall
%    and spreads only through cells whose colour ≠ wall.
%  * A zero cell that is reached becomes the colour of the flood‑fill
%    source (the source colour is the colour of the border cell that
%    started the flood).  A zero cell that cannot be reached becomes
%    wall+1.
%  * All other cells keep their original colour.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% -------- grid dimensions ------------------------------------------
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max{R : input(R, _, _)}.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max{C : input(_, C, _)}.
rows(Rcnt) :- Rcnt = max_row + 1.
cols(Ccnt) :- Ccnt = max_col + 1.

% -------- border cells (outermost row or column) ------------------
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = 0.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = max_row.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = 0.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = max_col.

% -------- border cells that are non‑zero -------------------------
border_nonzero(V) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% -------- smallest border colour (the “wall”) --------------------
wall(W) :- border_nonzero(W), #count{V : border_nonzero(V), V < W} = 0.

% -------- source cells (border cells whose colour ≠ wall) ---------
source(V,R,C) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != W.

% -------- cells that may be used in flood‑fill (colour ≠ wall) ----
allowed(R,C) :- input(R,C,X), X != W.

% -------- flood‑fill reachable cells (with source colour V) -------
reachable(V,R0,C0) :- source(V,R0,C0), allowed(R0,C0).
reachable(V,R2,C2) :- reachable(V,R1,C1), delta(DR,DC),
                       R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
                       allowed(R2,C2),
                       reachable(V,R1,C1).

% -------- zero cells that are reached by the flood‑fill ----------
zero_reachable(V,R,C) :- reachable(V,R,C), input(R,C,0).

% -------- any zero cell reached (exists a source) ----------------
any_zero(R,C) :- zero_reachable(V,R,C).

% -------- interior zero cells (zero not reachable) ----------------
interior_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), not any_zero(R,C).

% -------- output cell definition ----------------------------------
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

% Exactly one colour per position
1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% -------- 4‑connected neighbourhood offsets -----------------------
delta(0,1).   delta(0,-1).   delta(1,0).   delta(-1,0).

% -------- colour that interior zeros become (wall+1) ------------
wp1(WP1) :- wall(W), WP1 = W + 1.

% -------- constraints to enforce the correct colour ---------------
% 1. non‑zero cells keep their original colour
:- input(R,C,V), V != 0, output(R,C,Color), Color != V.

% 2. zero cells reached by the flood‑fill keep the source colour
:- zero_reachable(V,R,C), output(R,C,Color), Color != V.

% 3. interior zeros become wall+1
:- interior_zero(R,C), wp1(WP1), output(R,C,Color), Color != WP1.

<context>
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP).

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell contains a color value (integer 0–9). The goal is to discover the hidden transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.
</context>

<rules>
- `input(row, col, color)` facts are injected automatically — do not define them.
- `color(0..9)` facts are injected automatically — do not define them.
- the output predicate is always `output(row, col, color)` — do not redefine it.
- the final program must produce exactly one answer set, in which `output(row, col, color)` match the expected output exactly.
</rules>

<transformation>
The input is treated as a tile map. The output is NxM times larger than the input (where the input is N rows x M cols). Each input cell at (BR, BC) with a non-zero value causes a copy of the full input to be placed in the corresponding NxM block of the output (at rows BR*N .. BR*N+N-1, cols BC*M .. BC*M+M-1). Each input cell with value 0 produces an all-zero block at that position.
</transformation>

<asp>
% Count distinct row/col indices — equals grid dimensions for 0-indexed inputs
n_rows(N) :- N = #count{R : input(R, _, _)}.
n_cols(N) :- N = #count{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% Output is NR*NR rows and NC*NC cols (input tiled by itself)
output_cell(R, C) :-
    input(BR, BC, _), input(DR, DC, _),
    n_rows(NR), n_cols(NC),
    R = BR*NR + DR, C = BC*NC + DC.

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% Non-zero block: the sub-cell must equal the input value at the relative position
:- input(BR, BC, V), V != 0,
   input(DR, DC, TileColor),
   n_rows(NR), n_cols(NC),
   R = BR*NR + DR, C = BC*NC + DC,
   output(R, C, ActualColor), ActualColor != TileColor.

% Zero block: every sub-cell must be 0
:- input(BR, BC, 0),
   input(DR, DC, _),
   n_rows(NR), n_cols(NC),
   R = BR*NR + DR, C = BC*NC + DC,
   output(R, C, ActualColor), ActualColor != 0.
</asp>
</example_1>

<asp>
% Output has the same dimensions as the input
output_cell(R, C) :- input(R, C, _).
1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% Grid boundary coordinates
min_row(R) :- R = #min{Row : input(Row, _, _)}.
max_row(R) :- R = #max{Row : input(Row, _, _)}.
min_col(C) :- C = #min{Col : input(_, Col, _)}.
max_col(C) :- C = #max{Col : input(_, Col, _)}.

% 4-connected adjacency offsets
delta(0,1). delta(0,-1). delta(1,0). delta(-1,0).

% Non-3 cells on the grid boundary are reachable from outside
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, min_row(Rmin), R = Rmin.
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, max_row(Rmax), R = Rmax.
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, min_col(Cmin), C = Cmin.
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, max_col(Cmax), C = Cmax.

% Flood-fill outward through non-3 cells
outside(R2, C2) :-
    outside(R1, C1), delta(DR, DC),
    R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
    input(R2, C2, V), V != 3.

% Color-3 cells stay 3
:- input(R, C, 3), output(R, C, Color), Color != 3.

% Outside non-3 cells stay 0
:- outside(R, C), output(R, C, Color), Color != 0.

% Interior non-3 cells (enclosed by 3s, not reachable from boundary) become 4
:- input(R, C, 0), not outside(R, C), output(R, C, Color), Color != 4.
</asp>
</example_2>

The earlier successful example (example_grid_3) (the one with color 3 walls) used:

% Output has same dimensions as input
output_cell(R, C) :- input(R, C, _).
1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% Boundary
min_row(R) :- R = #min{R : input(R, _, _)}.
max_row(R) :- R = #max{R : input(R, _, _)}.
min_col(C) :- C = #min{C : input(_, C, _)}.
max_col(C) :- C = #max{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% 4-connected adjacency offsets
delta(0,1). delta(0,-1). delta(1,0). delta(-1,0).

% Outside (non-3 cells on border) etc.
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, min_row(Rmin), R = Rmin.
...
outside(R2, C2) :- outside(R1, C1), delta(DR, DC),
    R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
    input(R2, C2, V), V != 3.

% Color-3 cells stay 3
:- input(R, C, 3), output(R, C, Color), Color != 3.

% Outside non-3 cells stay 0
:- outside(R, C), output(R, C, Color), Color != 0.

% Interior non-3 cells (enclosed by 3s, not reachable from boundary) become 4
:- input(R, C, 0), not outside(R, C), output(R, C, Color), Color != 4.

% Minimum and maximum row/col

min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min{R : input(R, _, _)}.

min_col(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min{C : input(_, C, _)}.

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% Boundary zero cells (zero cells on border)

reachable(R,C) :- boundary_zero(R,C).
reachable(R2,C2) :- reachable(R1,C1), delta(DR,DC),
                   R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
                   input(R2,C2,0).

% Determine grid dimensions
min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min{R : input(R, _, _)}.
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max{R : input(R, _, _)}.
min_col(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min{C : input(_, C, _)}.

output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

% 4‑connected offsets

% Zero cells that touch the border are the start of the flood‑fill
boundary_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), R = Rmin.
boundary_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), R = Rmax.
boundary_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), C = Cmin.
boundary_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), C = Cmax.

% Flood‑fill through zero cells only

reachable(R2,C2) :- reachable(R1,C1), delta(DR,DC),
                   R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
                   input(R2,C2,0).

% Enforce the correct colour for every cell

% 2. reachable zeros stay 0

% 3. interior zeros become 3

% 4‑connected neighbourhood offsets
delta(0,1). delta(0,-1). delta(1,0). delta(-1,0).

boundary_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), R = Rmin.

<transformation>
The input is treated as a tile map. The output is NxM times larger than the input (where the input is N rows x M cols). Each input cell at (BR, BC) with a non-zero value causes a copy of the full input to be placed in the corresponding NxM block of the output (at rows BR*N .. BR*N+N-1, cols BC*M .. BC*M+M-1). Each input cell with value 0 produces an all-zero block at that position.
</transformation>

<asp>
% Count distinct row/col indices — equals grid dimensions for 0-indexed inputs
n_rows(N) :- N = #count{R : input(R, _, _)}.
n_cols(N) :- N = #count{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% Output is NR*NR rows and NC*NC cols (input tiled by itself)
output_cell(R, C) :-
    input(BR, BC, _), input(DR, DC, _),
    n_rows(NR), n_cols(NC),
    R = BR*NR + DR, C = BC*NC + DC.

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% Non-zero block: the sub-cell must equal the input value at the relative position
:- input(BR, BC, V), V != 0,
   input(DR, DC, TileColor),
   n_rows(NR), n_cols(NC),
   R = BR*NR + DR, C = BC*NC + DC,
   output(R, C, ActualColor), ActualColor != TileColor.

% Zero block: every sub-cell must be 0
:- input(BR, BC, 0),
   input(DR, DC, _),
   n_rows(NR), n_cols(NC),
   R = BR*NR + DR, C = BC*NC + DC,
   output(R, C, ActualColor), ActualColor != 0.
</asp>
</example_1>

<transformation>
The output is the same size as the input. Color-3 cells are unchanged. Non-3 cells (value 0) that are enclosed by color-3 walls — i.e., cannot reach the grid boundary via 4-connected movement through non-3 cells — are filled with color 4. All other non-3 cells remain 0.

<asp>
% Output has the same dimensions as the input
output_cell(R, C) :- input(R, C, _).
1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% Grid boundary coordinates
min_row(R) :- R = #min{Row : input(Row, _, _)}.
max_row(R) :- R = #max{Row : input(Row, _, _)}.
min_col(C) :- C = #min{Col : input(_, Col, _)}.
max_col(C) :- C = #max{Col : input(_, Col, _)}.

% 4-connected adjacency offsets
delta(0,1). delta(0,-1). delta(1,0). delta(-1,0).

% Non-3 cells on the grid boundary are reachable from outside
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, min_row(Rmin), R = Rmin.
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, max_row(Rmax), R = Rmax.
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, min_col(Cmin), C = Cmin.
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, max_col(Cmax), C = Cmax.

% Flood-fill outward through non-3 cells
outside(R2, C2) :-
    outside(R1, C1), delta(DR, DC),
    R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
    input(R2, C2, V), V != 3.

% Color-3 cells stay 3
:- input(R, C, 3), output(R, C, Color), Color != 3.

% Outside non-3 cells stay 0
:- outside(R, C), output(R, C, Color), Color != 0.

% Interior non-3 cells (enclosed by 3s, not reachable from boundary) become 4
:- input(R, C, 0), not outside(R, C), output(R, C, Color), Color != 4.
</asp>

RowMax(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max{R : input(R, _, _)}.

ColMax(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% Identify border cells

boundary(R,C) :- R = Rmax.
boundary(R,C) :- C = Cmin.
boundary(R,C) :- C = Cmax.

output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

boundary(R,C) :- R = Rmax.

% Non‑zero colours on the border

% Wall colour = smallest border non‑zero colour
wall(W) :- #min{W : border_nonzero(W)}.

% Fill colour = wall + 1

% Directions

% Cells that are interior zeros (zero not reachable)

% Exactly one output per position
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

% Determine bounds
min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min{R : input(R, _, _)}.
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max{R : input(R, _, _)}.
min_col(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min{C : input(_, C, _)}.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% Border definition
boundary(R,C) :- R = Rmin.
boundary(R,C) :- R = Rmax.
boundary(R,C) :- C = Cmin.
boundary(R,C) :- C = Cmax.

% Non‑zero colours on the border
border_nonzero(V) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% Smallest border non‑zero colour (wall)
wall(W) :- #min{W : border_nonzero(W)}.

% Flood‑fill through cells that are not the wall colour
start(R,C) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != W.
reachable(R2,C2) :- reachable(R1,C1), delta(DR,DC), R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC, input(R2,C2,V), V != W.
reachable(R,C) :- start(R,C).

% Zero cells that are interior (cannot be reached)
interior_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), not reachable(R,C).

% Output rules
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0.        % keep non‑zero colour
output(R,C,0) :- input(R,C,0), reachable(R,C). % zeros that can reach border stay 0
output(R,C,X) :- input(R,C,0), interior_zero(R,C), X = W + 1. % interior zeros become wall+1

% Exactly one colour per cell
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

<asp>
% Determine grid dimensions
min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min{R : input(R, _, _)}.
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max{R : input(R, _, _)}.
min_col(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min{C : input(_, C, _)}.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% Border definition
boundary(R,C) :- R = Rmin.
boundary(R,C) :- R = Rmax.
boundary(R,C) :- C = Cmin.
boundary(R,C) :- C = Cmax.

% Non‑zero colours on the border
border_nonzero(V) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% Smallest border non‑zero colour (wall colour)

% Determine the grid dimensions
min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min{R : input(R, _, _)}.
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max{R : input(R, _, _)}.
min_col(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min{C : input(_, C, _)}.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% Border cells (any cell on the outermost row or column)
boundary(R,C) :- R = Rmin.
boundary(R,C) :- R = Rmax.
boundary(R,C) :- C = Cmin.
boundary(R,C) :- C = Cmax.

% Non‑zero colours that appear on the border
border_nonzero(V) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% Smallest border colour (the “wall” colour)
wall(W) :- #min{W : border_nonzero(W)}.

% Flood‑fill through cells that are not the wall colour
start(R,C) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != W.
reachable(R2,C2) :- reachable(R1,C1), delta(DR,DC),
                   R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
                   input(R2,C2,V), V != W.
reachable(R,C) :- start(R,C).

% Zero cells that are interior (cannot reach the border)
interior_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), not reachable(R,C).

% Output rules
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0.                % keep a non‑zero colour
output(R,C,0) :- input(R,C,0), reachable(R,C).        % zeros that can reach the border stay 0
output(R,C,X) :- input(R,C,0), interior_zero(R,C), X = W + 1. % interior zeros become wall+1

% Exactly one colour per position
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% Offsets for 4‑connected neighbourhoods
delta(0,1). delta(0,-1). delta(1,0). delta(-1,0).
</asp>

% Determine the grid dimensions
min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min{R : input(R, _, _)}.
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max{R : input(R, _, _)}.
min_col(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min{C : input(_, C, _)}.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% Border cells (any cell on the outermost row or column)
boundary(R,C) :- R = Rmin.
boundary(R,C) :- R = Rmax.
boundary(R,C) :- C = Cmin.
boundary(R,C) :- C = Cmax.

% Non‑zero colours that appear on the border
border_nonzero(V) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% Smallest border colour (the “wall” colour)
wall(W) :- #min{W : border_nonzero(W)}.

% Flood‑fill through cells that are not the wall colour
start(R,C) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != W.
reachable(R2,C2) :- reachable(R1,C1), delta(DR,DC),
                   R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
                   input(R2,C2,V), V != W.
reachable(R,C) :- start(R,C).

% Zero cells that are interior (cannot reach the border)
interior_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), not reachable(R,C).

% Output rules
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0.                % keep a non‑zero colour
output(R,C,0) :- input(R,C,0), reachable(R,C).        % zeros that can reach the border stay 0
output(R,C,X) :- input(R,C,0), interior_zero(R,C), X = W + 1. % interior zeros become wall+1

% Exactly one colour per position
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% Offsets for 4‑connected neighbourhoods
delta(0,1). delta(0,-1). delta(1,0). delta(-1,0).

<context>
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP).

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell contains a color value (integer 0–9). The goal is to discover the hidden transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.
</context>

<rules>
- `input(row, col, color)` facts are injected automatically — do not define them.
- `color(0..9)` facts are injected automatically — do not define them.
- the output predicate is always `output(row, col, color)` — do not redefine it.
- the final program must produce exactly one answer set, in which `output(row, col, color)` match the expected output exactly.
</rules>

Below are two fully worked examples.

</example_grid_1>

<transformation>
The input is treated as a tile map. The output is NxM times larger than the input (where the input is N rows x M cols). Each input cell at (BR, BC) with a non-zero value causes a copy of the full input to be placed in the corresponding NxM block of the output (at rows BR*N .. BR*N+N-1, cols BC*M .. BC*M+M-1). Each input cell with value 0 produces an all-zero block at that position.
</transformation>

<asp>
% Count distinct row/col indices — equals grid dimensions for 0-indexed inputs
n_rows(N) :- N = #count{R : input(R, _, _)}.
n_cols(N) :- N = #count{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% Output is NR*NR rows and NC*NC cols (input tiled by itself)
output_cell(R, C) :-
    input(BR, BC, _), input(DR, DC, _),
    n_rows(NR), n_cols(NC),
    R = BR*NR + DR, C = BC*NC + DC.

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% Non-zero block: the sub-cell must equal the input value at the relative position
:- input(BR, BC, V), V != 0,
   input(DR, DC, TileColor),
   n_rows(NR), n_cols(NC),
   R = BR*NR + DR, C = BC*NC + DC,
   output(R, C, ActualColor), ActualColor != TileColor.

% Zero block: every sub-cell must be 0
:- input(BR, BC, 0),
   input(DR, DC, _),
   n_rows(NR), n_cols(NC),
   R = BR*NR + DR, C = BC*NC + DC,
   output(R, C, ActualColor), ActualColor != 0.
</asp>
</example_1>

<transformation>
The output is the same size as the input. Color-3 cells are unchanged. Non-3 cells (value 0) that are enclosed by color-3 walls — i.e., cannot reach the grid boundary via 4-connected movement through non-3 cells — are filled with color 4. All other non-3 cells remain 0.
</transformation>

<asp>
% Output has the same dimensions as the input
output_cell(R, C) :- input(R, C, _).
1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% Grid boundary coordinates
min_row(R) :- R = #min{Row : input(Row, _, _)}.
max_row(R) :- R = #max{Row : input(Row, _, _)}.
min_col(C) :- C = #min{Col : input(_, Col, _)}.
max_col(C) :- C = #max{Col : input(_, Col, _)}.

% 4-connected adjacency offsets
delta(0,1). delta(0,-1). delta(1,0). delta(-1,0).

% Non-3 cells on the grid boundary are reachable from outside
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, min_row(Rmin), R = Rmin.
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, max_row(Rmax), R = Rmax.
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, min_col(Cmin), C = Cmin.
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, max_col(Cmax), C = Cmax.

% Flood-fill outward through non-3 cells
outside(R2, C2) :-
    outside(R1, C1), delta(DR, DC),
    R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
    input(R2, C2, V), V != 3.

% Color-3 cells stay 3
:- input(R, C, 3), output(R, C, Color), Color != 3.

% Outside non-3 cells stay 0
:- outside(R, C), output(R, C, Color), Color != 0.

% Interior non-3 cells (enclosed by 3s, not reachable from boundary) become 4
:- input(R, C, 0), not outside(R, C), output(R, C, Color), Color != 4.
</asp>
</example_2>

<asp>
% Determine the grid dimensions
min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min{R : input(R, _, _)}.
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max{R : input(R, _, _)}.
min_col(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min{C : input(_, C, _)}.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% Border cells (any cell on the outermost row or column)
boundary(R,C) :- R = Rmin.
boundary(R,C) :- R = Rmax.
boundary(R,C) :- C = Cmin.
boundary(R,C) :- C = Cmax.

% Non‑zero colours that appear on the border
border_nonzero(V) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% Smallest border colour (the “wall” colour)
wall(W) :- #min{W : border_nonzero(W)}.

% Flood‑fill through cells that are not the wall colour
start(R,C) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != W.
reachable(R2,C2) :- reachable(R1,C1), delta(DR,DC),
                   R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
                   input(R2,C2,V), V != W.
reachable(R,C) :- start(R,C).

% Zero cells that are interior (cannot reach the border)
interior_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), not reachable(R,C).

% Output rules
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0.                % keep a non‑zero colour
output(R,C,0) :- input(R,C,0), reachable(R,C).        % zeros that can reach the border stay 0
output(R,C,X) :- input(R,C,0), interior_zero(R,C), X = W + 1. % interior zeros become wall+1

% Exactly one colour per position
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% Offsets for 4‑connected neighbourhoods
delta(0,1). delta(0,-1). delta(1,0). delta(-1,0).
</asp>

% Determine the grid dimensions
min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min{R : input(R, _, _)}.
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max{R : input(R, _, _)}.
min_col(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min{C : input(_, C, _)}.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% Border cells (any cell on the outermost row or column)
boundary(R,C) :- R = Rmin.
boundary(R,C) :- R = Rmax.
boundary(R,C) :- C = Cmin.
boundary(R,C) :- C = Cmax.

% Non‑zero colours that appear on the border
border_nonzero(V) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% Smallest border colour (the “wall” colour)
wall(W) :- #min{W : border_nonzero(W)}.

% Flood‑fill through cells that are not the wall colour
start(R,C) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != W.
reachable(R2,C2) :- reachable(R1,C1), delta(DR,DC),
                   R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
                   input(R2,C2,V), V != W.
reachable(R,C) :- start(R,C).

% Output rules
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0.                % keep a non‑zero colour
output(R,C,0) :- input(R,C,0), reachable(R,C).        % zeros that can reach the border stay 0
output(R,C,X) :- input(R,C,0), interior_zero(R,C), X = W + 1. % interior zeros become wall+1

% Exactly one colour per position
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% Offsets for 4‑connected neighbourhoods
delta(0,1). delta(0,-1). delta(1,0). delta(-1,0).

refinements.response:
```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI transformation:
%  * Find the smallest colour on the outer border (the “wall”)
%    – every non‑zero cell on the border with colour ≠ wall starts a flood‑fill.
%  * The flood‑fill spreads only through cells that are NOT the wall colour.
%    * A zero cell that is reached by a flood‑fill whose source colour is V
%      becomes colour V (the source colour – 0 if the source is a border zero).
%    * Zero cells that cannot be reached become wall+1.
%  * All other cells keep their original colour.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid dimensions -------------------------------------------------
row_min(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min{R : input(R, _, _)}.
row_max(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max{R : input(R, _, _)}.
col_min(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min{C : input(_, C, _)}.
col_max(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% ----- border cells ----------------------------------------------------
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = Rmin.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = Rmax.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = Cmin.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = Cmax.

% ----- colour that appears on the border (non‑zero) --------------------
border_nonzero(Col) :- input(R,C,Col), boundary(R,C), Col != 0.

% ----- smallest border colour (the “wall”) ---------------------------
wall(W) :- border_nonzero(W), not border_nonzero(C), C < W.

% ----- start cells for the flood‑fill (border cells whose colour ≠ wall) --
start(V,R,C) :- input(R,C,V), boundary(R,C), V != W.

% ----- flood‑fill through cells that are not the wall colour ----------
% base case: the start cells themselves
reachable(V,R0,C0) :- start(V,R0,C0), input(R0,C0,X), X != W.

% propagation step
reachable(V,R2,C2) :- reachable(V,R1,C1), delta(DR,DC),
                       R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
                       input(R2,C2,X), X != W,
                       reachable(V,R1,C1).

% ----- zero cells that are reached by a flood‑fill --------------------
reachable_zero(V,R,C) :- reachable(V,R,C), input(R,C,0).

% there is at least one reachable zero for a cell
any_zero(R,C) :- reachable_zero(V,R,C).

% interior zero cells (cannot be reached from the border)
interior_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), not any_zero(R,C).

% ----- output for the three kinds of cells ---------------------------
% 1. non‑zero cells keep their original colour
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% 2. zero cells that are reachable become the colour of the flood‑fill source
output(R,C,V) :- reachable_zero(V,R,C).

% 3. interior zero cells become wall+1
output(R,C,Color) :- interior_zero(R,C), Color = W+1.

% ----- exactly one colour per position ----------------------------------
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% ----- 4‑connected neighbourhood offsets -------------------------------
delta(0,1).   delta(0,-1).   delta(1,0).   delta(-1,0).

% ----- constraints to force the correct colour -------------------------
% 1. non‑zero cells keep their original colour
:- input(R,C,V), V != 0, output(R,C,Color), Color != V.

% 2. reachable zero cells keep the colour of the flood‑fill source
:- reachable_zero(V,R,C), output(R,C,Color), Color != V.

% 3. interior zero cells become wall+1
:- interior_zero(R,C), output(R,C,Color), Color != W+1.
</asp>

%  ARC‑AGI transformation:
%  * Find the smallest colour on the outer border (the “wall”)
%    – every non‑zero cell on the border with colour ≠ wall starts a flood‑fill.
%  * The flood‑fill spreads only through cells that are NOT the wall colour.
%    * A zero cell that is reached by a flood‑fill whose source colour is V
%      becomes colour V (the source colour – 0 if the source is a border zero).
%    * Zero cells that cannot be reached become wall+1.
%  * All other cells keep their original colour.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid dimensions -------------------------------------------------
row_min(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min{R : input(R, _, _)}.
row_max(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max{R : input(R, _, _)}.
col_min(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min{C : input(_, C, _)}.
col_max(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% ----- border cells ----------------------------------------------------
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = Rmin.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = Rmax.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = Cmin.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = Cmax.

% ----- colour that appears on the border (non‑zero) --------------------
border_nonzero(Col) :- input(R,C,Col), boundary(R,C), Col != 0.

% ----- smallest border colour (the “wall”) ---------------------------
wall(W) :- border_nonzero(W), not border_nonzero(C), C < W.

% ----- start cells for the flood‑fill (border cells whose colour ≠ wall) --
start(V,R,C) :- input(R,C,V), boundary(R,C), V != W.

% ----- flood‑fill through cells that are not the wall colour ----------
% base case: the start cells themselves
reachable(V,R0,C0) :- start(V,R0,C0), input(R0,C0,X), X != W.

% propagation step
reachable(V,R2,C2) :- reachable(V,R1,C1), delta(DR,DC),
                       R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
                       input(R2,C2,X), X != W,
                       reachable(V,R1,C1).

% ----- zero cells that are reached by a flood‑fill --------------------
reachable_zero(V,R,C) :- reachable(V,R,C), input(R,C,0).

% there is at least one reachable zero for a cell

% interior zero cells (cannot be reached from the border)

% ----- output for the three kinds of cells ---------------------------
% 1. non‑zero cells keep their original colour
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% 2. zero cells that are reachable become the colour of the flood‑fill source
output(R,C,V) :- reachable_zero(V,R,C).

% 3. interior zero cells become wall+1
output(R,C,Color) :- interior_zero(R,C), Color = W+1.

% ----- exactly one colour per position ----------------------------------
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% ----- 4‑connected neighbourhood offsets -------------------------------
delta(0,1).   delta(0,-1).   delta(1,0).   delta(-1,0).

% ----- constraints to force the correct colour -------------------------
% 1. non‑zero cells keep their original colour
:- input(R,C,V), V != 0, output(R,C,Color), Color != V.

% 2. reachable zero cells keep the colour of the flood‑fill source

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell contains a color value (integer 0–9). The goal is to discover the hidden transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<transformation>
The input is treated as a tile map. The output is NxM times larger than the input (where the input is N rows x M cols). Each input cell at (BR, BC) with a non-zero value causes a copy of the full input to be placed in the corresponding NxM block of the output (at rows BR*N .. BR*N+N-1, cols BC*M .. BC*M+M-1). Each input cell with value 0 produces an all-zero block at that position.
</transformation>

<asp>
% Count distinct row/col indices — equals grid dimensions for 0-indexed inputs
n_rows(N) :- N = #count{R : input(R, _, _)}.
n_cols(N) :- N = #count{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% Output is NR*NR rows and NC*NC cols (input tiled by itself)
output_cell(R, C) :-
    input(BR, BC, _), input(DR, DC, _),
    n_rows(NR), n_cols(NC),
    R = BR*NR + DR, C = BC*NC + DC.

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% Non-zero block: the sub-cell must equal the input value at the relative position
:- input(BR, BC, V), V != 0,
   input(DR, DC, TileColor),
   n_rows(NR), n_cols(NC),
   R = BR*NR + DR, C = BC*NC + DC,
   output(R, C, ActualColor), ActualColor != TileColor.

% Zero block: every sub-cell must be 0
:- input(BR, BC, 0),
   input(DR, DC, _),
   n_rows(NR), n_cols(NC),
   R = BR*NR + DR, C = BC*NC + DC,
   output(R, C, ActualColor), ActualColor != 0.
</asp>
</example_1>

<transformation>
The output is the same size as the input. Color-3 cells are unchanged. Non-3 cells (value 0) that are enclosed by color-3 walls — i.e., cannot reach the grid boundary via 4-connected movement through non-3 cells — are filled with color 4. All other non-3 cells remain 0.
</transformation>

<asp>
% Output has the same dimensions as the input
output_cell(R, C) :- input(R, C, _).
1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% Grid boundary coordinates
min_row(R) :- R = #min{Row : input(Row, _, _)}.
max_row(R) :- R = #max{Row : input(Row, _, _)}.
min_col(C) :- C = #min{Col : input(_, Col, _)}.
max_col(C) :- C = #max{Col : input(_, Col, _)}.

% 4-connected adjacency offsets
delta(0,1). delta(0,-1). delta(1,0). delta(-1,0).

% Non-3 cells on the grid boundary are reachable from outside
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, min_row(Rmin), R = Rmin.
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, max_row(Rmax), R = Rmax.
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, min_col(Cmin), C = Cmin.
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, max_col(Cmax), C = Cmax.

% Flood-fill outward through non-3 cells
outside(R2, C2) :-
    outside(R1, C1), delta(DR, DC),
    R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
    input(R2, C2, V), V != 3.

% Color-3 cells stay 3
:- input(R, C, 3), output(R, C, Color), Color != 3.

% Outside non-3 cells stay 0
:- outside(R, C), output(R, C, Color), Color != 0.

% Interior non-3 cells (enclosed by 3s, not reachable from boundary) become 4
:- input(R, C, 0), not outside(R, C), output(R, C, Color), Color != 4.
</asp>
</example_2>

% Determine the grid dimensions
min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min{R : input(R, _, _)}.
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max{R : input(R, _, _)}.
min_col(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min{C : input(_, C, _)}.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% Border cells (any cell on the outermost row or column)
boundary(R,C) :- R = Rmin.
boundary(R,C) :- R = Rmax.
boundary(R,C) :- C = Cmin.
boundary(R,C) :- C = Cmax.

% Non‑zero colours that appear on the border
border_nonzero(V) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% Smallest border colour (the “wall” colour)
wall(W) :- #min{W : border_nonzero(W)}.

% Flood‑fill through cells that are not the wall colour
start(R,C) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != W.
reachable(R2,C2) :- reachable(R1,C1), delta(DR,DC),
                   R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
                   input(R2,C2,V), V != W.
reachable(R,C) :- start(R,C).

% Zero cells that are interior (cannot reach the border)
interior_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), not reachable(R,C).

% Output rules
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0.                % keep a non‑zero colour
output(R,C,0) :- input(R,C,0), reachable(R,C).        % zeros that can reach the border stay 0
output(R,C,X) :- input(R,C,0), interior_zero(R,C), X = W + 1. % interior zeros become wall+1

% Exactly one colour per position
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% Offsets for 4‑connected neighbourhoods
delta(0,1). delta(0,-1). delta(1,0). delta(-1,0).
</asp>

<asp>
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI transformation:
%  * Find the smallest colour on the outer border (the “wall”)
%    – every non‑zero cell on the border with colour ≠ wall starts a flood‑fill.
%  * The flood‑fill spreads only through cells that are NOT the wall colour.
%    * A zero cell that is reached by a flood‑fill whose source colour is V
%      becomes colour V (the source colour – 0 if the source is a border zero).
%    * Zero cells that cannot be reached become wall+1.
%  * All other cells keep their original colour.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid dimensions -------------------------------------------------
row_min(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min{R : input(R, _, _)}.
row_max(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max{R : input(R, _, _)}.
col_min(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min{C : input(_, C, _)}.
col_max(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% ----- border cells ----------------------------------------------------
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = Rmin.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = Rmax.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = Cmin.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = Cmax.

% ----- colour that appears on the border (non‑zero) --------------------
border_nonzero(Col) :- input(R,C,Col), boundary(R,C), Col != 0.

% ----- smallest border colour (the “wall”) ---------------------------
wall(W) :- border_nonzero(W), not border_nonzero(C), C < W.

% ----- start cells for the flood‑fill (border cells whose colour ≠ wall) --
start(V,R,C) :- input(R,C,V), boundary(R,C), V != W.

% ----- flood‑fill through cells that are not the wall colour ----------
% base case: the start cells themselves
reachable(V,R0,C0) :- start(V,R0,C0), input(R0,C0,X), X != W.

% propagation step
reachable(V,R2,C2) :- reachable(V,R1,C1), delta(DR,DC),
                       R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
                       input(R2,C2,X), X != W,
                       reachable(V,R1,C1).

% ----- zero cells that are reached by a flood‑fill --------------------
reachable_zero(V,R,C) :- reachable(V,R,C), input(R,C,0).

% there is at least one reachable zero for a cell
any_zero(R,C) :- reachable_zero(V,R,C).

% interior zero cells (cannot be reached from the border)
interior_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), not any_zero(R,C).

% ----- output for the three kinds of cells ---------------------------
% 1. non‑zero cells keep their original colour
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% 2. zero cells that are reachable become the colour of the flood‑fill source
output(R,C,V) :- reachable_zero(V,R,C).

% 3. interior zero cells become wall+1
output(R,C,Color) :- interior_zero(R,C), Color = W+1.

% ----- exactly one colour per position ----------------------------------
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% ----- 4‑connected neighbourhood offsets -------------------------------
delta(0,1).   delta(0,-1).   delta(1,0).   delta(-1,0).

% ----- constraints to force the correct colour -------------------------
% 1. non‑zero cells keep their original colour
:- input(R,C,V), V != 0, output(R,C,Color), Color != V.

% 2. reachable zero cells keep the colour of the flood‑fill source
:- reachable_zero(V,R,C), output(R,C,Color), Color != V.

% 3. interior zero cells become wall+1
:- interior_zero(R,C), output(R,C,Color), Color != W+1.
</asp>

% Compute dimensions
row_cnt(Rcnt) :- Rcnt = #count{R : input(R, _, _)}.
col_cnt(Ccnt) :- Ccnt = #count{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% Border predicate
boundary(R,C) :-
    input(R,C,_), (R = 0) | (R = Rcnt-1) | (C = 0) | (C = Ccnt-1).

% Border non-zero colour predicate
border_nonzero(V) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% Choose wall colour (smallest border colour)
wall(W) :- border_nonzero(W), #count{V : border_nonzero(V), V < W} = 0.

% Flood fill start cells (border cells not wall)
start(R,C) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != W.

% Flood fill reachable cells (cannot pass through wall cells)
reachable(R,C) :- start(R,C).
reachable(R2,C2) :- reachable(R1,C1), delta(DR,DC), R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
    input(R2,C2,Col), Col != W,
    reachable(R1,C1).

% Zero cells reachable
reachable_zero(R,C) :- reachable(R,C), input(R,C,0).

% Interior zero cells (zero not reachable)
interior_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), not reachable(R,C).

% Non-zero cells keep original colour
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% Exactly one output per position
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% Define counts
row_cnt(Rcnt) :- Rcnt = #count{R : input(R, _, _)}.
col_cnt(Ccnt) :- Ccnt = #count{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% Boundary definition
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = 0.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = Rcnt-1.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = 0.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = Ccnt-1.

% Border non-zero colour
border_nonzero(V) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% Choose wall colour (smallest border non-zero)
wall(W) :- border_nonzero(W), #count{V : border_nonzero(V), V < W} = 0.

% Ensure exactly one wall (choice)
1 { wall(W) : color(W) } 1 :- border_nonzero(W).

% Ensure W is smallest border colour (constraint)
:- wall(W), #count{V : border_nonzero(V), V < W} = 0.

% Flood fill start cells (border cells not wall)
reachable_any(R,C) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != W, wall(W).

% Flood fill propagation (cannot step onto wall colour)
reachable_any(R2,C2) :- reachable_any(R1,C1), delta(DR,DC), R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
    input(R2,C2,Col), Col != W, wall(W).

% Zero cells reachable (and zero)
reachable_zero(R,C) :- reachable_any(R,C), input(R,C,0).

% Interior zero cells (zero not reachable)
interior_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), not reachable_zero(R,C).

% Output definitions (exactly one per cell)
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% Offsets
delta(0,1). delta(0,-1). delta(1,0). delta(-1,0).

% Constraints to enforce correct colour per cell

% 1. Non‑zero cells keep original colour
:- input(R,C,V), V != 0, output(R,C,Color), Color != V.

% 2. Reachable zero cells become 0
:- reachable(R,C), input(R,C,0), output(R,C,Color), Color != 0.

% 3. Interior zero cells become wall+1
:- interior_zero(R,C), output(R,C,Color), wall(W), W != 0, Color != W+1.

```asp
% Determine number of rows and columns
row_cnt(Rcnt) :- Rcnt = #max{R : input(R, _, _)} + 1. % Actually row indices start at 0, so number of rows = max index + 1
col_cnt(Ccnt) :- Ccnt = #max{C : input(_, C, _)} + 1.

% Define boundary (outermost rows/columns)
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = 0.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = Rcnt - 1.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = 0.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = Ccnt - 1.

% Border non‑zero colours
border_nonzero(V) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% Choose wall colour (smallest border non‑zero colour)
1 { wall(W) : color(W) } 1 :- border_nonzero(W).

% Enforce that W is the smallest border colour
:- wall(W), #count{V : border_nonzero(V), V < W} = 0.

% Flood fill start cells (border cells whose colour is not the wall)
reachable_any(R,C) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != W, wall(W).

% Flood fill propagation (cannot step on wall colour)
reachable_any(R2,C2) :- reachable_any(R1,C1), delta(DR,DC), R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
    input(R2,C2,Col), Col != W, wall(W).

% Zero cells that are reachable become 0
reachable_zero(R,C) :- reachable_any(R,C), input(R,C,0).

% Zero cells that are not reachable become interior
interior_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), not reachable_zero(R,C).

% Exactly one colour per position
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% Offsets for 4‑connected neighbourhoods
delta(0,1). delta(0,-1). delta(1,0). delta(-1,0).

% Constraints to enforce correct colour
% 1. Non‑zero cells keep original colour
:- input(R,C,V), V != 0, output(R,C,Color), Color != V.

% 2. Reachable zero cells become 0
:- reachable_any(R,C), input(R,C,0), output(R,C,Color), Color != 0.

% 3. Interior zero cells become wall+1
:- interior_zero(R,C), output(R,C,Color), wall(W), W != 0, Color != W+1.
</asp>
```

refinements.response:
```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI transformation:
%  * Find the smallest colour on the outer border (the “wall”).
%  * Flood‑fill from every border cell whose colour is NOT the wall,
%    moving only through cells whose colour ≠ wall.
%  * A zero cell that is reached by this flood‑fill stays 0.
%  * A zero cell that cannot be reached becomes wall+1.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid dimensions -------------------------------------------------
row_cnt(Rcnt) :- Rcnt = #max{R : input(R, _, _)} + 1.
col_cnt(Ccnt) :- Ccnt = #max{C : input(_, C, _)} + 1.

% ----- outer border (any cell on the outermost row or column) ----------
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = 0.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = Rcnt - 1.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = 0.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = Ccnt - 1.

% ----- border non‑zero colours ------------------------------------------
border_nonzero(V) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% ----- choose the wall colour (smallest border non‑zero colour) ---------
1 { wall(W) : color(W) } 1 :- border_nonzero(W).

% enforce that W is the smallest border colour (no smaller border colour exists)
:- wall(W), #count{V : border_nonzero(V), V < W} = 0.

% ----- flood‑fill start cells (border cells whose colour ≠ wall) ----------
reachable_any(R,C) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != W, wall(W).

% propagation: move to 4‑neighbours, staying away from wall cells ----------
reachable_any(R2,C2) :- reachable_any(R1,C1), delta(DR,DC),
                       R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
                       input(R2,C2,Col), Col != W, wall(W).

% zero cells that are reachable become 0
reachable_zero(R,C) :- reachable_any(R,C), input(R,C,0).

% zero cells that are not reachable become interior zeros
interior_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), not reachable_zero(R,C).

% ----- exactly one colour per position ------------------------------------
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% ----- 4‑connected neighbourhood offsets ---------------------------------
delta(0,1).   delta(0,-1).   delta(1,0).   delta(-1,0).

% ----- constraints to enforce the correct colour for each cell -------------
% 1. non‑zero cells keep their original colour
:- input(R,C,V), V != 0, output(R,C,Color), Color != V.

% 2. reachable zero cells stay 0
:- reachable_any(R,C), input(R,C,0), output(R,C,Color), Color != 0.

% 3. interior zero cells become wall+1
:- interior_zero(R,C), output(R,C,Color), wall(W), W != 0, Color != W+1.
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI transformation:
%  * Find the smallest colour on the outer border (the “wall”).
%  * Flood‑fill from every border cell whose colour is NOT the wall,
%    moving only through cells whose colour ≠ wall.
%  * A zero cell that is reached by this flood‑fill stays 0.
%  * A zero cell that cannot be reached becomes wall+1.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid dimensions -------------------------------------------------
row_cnt(Rcnt) :- Rcnt = #max{R : input(R, _, _)} + 1.
col_cnt(Ccnt) :- Ccnt = #max{C : input(_, C, _)} + 1.

% ----- outer border (any cell on the outermost row or column) ----------
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = 0.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = Rcnt - 1.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = 0.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = Ccnt - 1.

% ----- border non‑zero colours ------------------------------------------
border_nonzero(V) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% ----- choose the wall colour (smallest border non‑zero colour) ---------
1 { wall(W) : color(W) } 1 :- border_nonzero(W).

% enforce that W is the smallest border colour (no smaller border colour exists)
:- wall(W), #count{V : border_nonzero(V), V < W} = 0.

% ----- flood‑fill start cells (border cells whose colour ≠ wall) ----------
reachable_any(R,C) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != W, wall(W).

% propagation: move to 4‑neighbours, staying away from wall cells ----------
reachable_any(R2,C2) :- reachable_any(R1,C1), delta(DR,DC),
                       R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
                       input(R2,C2,Col), Col != W, wall(W).

% zero cells that are reachable become 0
reachable_zero(R,C) :- reachable_any(R,C), input(R,C,0).

% zero cells that are not reachable become interior zeros
interior_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), not reachable_zero(R,C).

% ----- exactly one colour per position ------------------------------------
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% ----- 4‑connected neighbourhood offsets ---------------------------------
delta(0,1).   delta(0,-1).   delta(1,0).   delta(-1,0).

% ----- constraints to enforce the correct colour for each cell -------------
% 1. non‑zero cells keep their original colour
:- input(R,C,V), V != 0, output(R,C,Color), Color != V.

% 2. reachable zero cells stay 0
:- reachable_any(R,C), input(R,C,0), output(R,C,Color), Color != 0.

% 3. interior zero cells become wall+1
:- interior_zero(R,C), output(R,C,Color), wall(W), W != 0, Color != W+1.

<transformation>
The input is treated as a tile map. The output is NxM times larger than the input (where the input is N rows x M cols). Each input cell at (BR, BC) with a non-zero value causes a copy of the full input to be placed in the corresponding NxM block of the output (at rows BR*N .. BR*N+N-1, cols BC*M .. BC*M+M-1). Each input cell with value 0 produces an all-zero block at that position.
</transformation>

<asp>
% Count distinct row/col indices — equals grid dimensions for 0-indexed inputs
n_rows(N) :- N = #count{R : input(R, _, _)}.
n_cols(N) :- N = #count{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% Output is NR*NR rows and NC*NC cols (input tiled by itself)
output_cell(R, C) :-
    input(BR, BC, _), input(DR, DC, _),
    n_rows(NR), n_cols(NC),
    R = BR*NR + DR, C = BC*NC + DC.

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% Non-zero block: the sub-cell must equal the input value at the relative position
:- input(BR, BC, V), V != 0,
   input(DR, DC, TileColor),
   n_rows(NR), n_cols(NC),
   R = BR*NR + DR, C = BC*NC + DC,
   output(R, C, ActualColor), ActualColor != TileColor.

% Zero block: every sub-cell must be 0
:- input(BR, BC, 0),
   input(DR, DC, _),
   n_rows(NR), n_cols(NC),
   R = BR*NR + DR, C = BC*NC + DC,
   output(R, C, ActualColor), ActualColor != 0.
</asp>
</example_1>

<asp>
% Output has the same dimensions as the input
output_cell(R, C) :- input(R, C, _).
1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% Grid boundary coordinates
min_row(R) :- R = #min{Row : input(Row, _, _)}.
max_row(R) :- R = #max{Row : input(Row, _, _)}.
min_col(C) :- C = #min{Col : input(_, Col, _)}.
max_col(C) :- C = #max{Col : input(_, Col, _)}.

% 4-connected adjacency offsets
delta(0,1). delta(0,-1). delta(1,0). delta(-1,0).

% Non-3 cells on the grid boundary are reachable from outside
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, min_row(Rmin), R = Rmin.
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, max_row(Rmax), R = Rmax.
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, min_col(Cmin), C = Cmin.
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, max_col(Cmax), C = Cmax.

% Flood-fill outward through non-3 cells
outside(R2, C2) :-
    outside(R1, C1), delta(DR, DC),
    R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
    input(R2, C2, V), V != 3.

% Color-3 cells stay 3
:- input(R, C, 3), output(R, C, Color), Color != 3.

% Outside non-3 cells stay 0
:- outside(R, C), output(R, C, Color), Color != 0.

% Interior non-3 cells (enclosed by 3s, not reachable from boundary) become 4
:- input(R, C, 0), not outside(R, C), output(R, C, Color), Color != 4.
</asp>
</example_2>

min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min{R : input(R, _, _)}.

min_col(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% Border cells (any cell on the outermost row or column)
boundary(R,C) :- R = Rmin.
boundary(R,C) :- R = Rmax.
boundary(R,C) :- C = Cmin.
boundary(R,C) :- C = Cmax.

% Non‑zero colours that appear on the border
border_nonzero(V) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% Smallest border colour (the “wall” colour)
wall(W) :- #min{W : border_nonzero(W)}.

% Flood‑fill through cells that are not the wall colour
start(R,C) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != W.
reachable(R2,C2) :- reachable(R1,C1), delta(DR,DC),
                   R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
                   input(R2,C2,V), V != W.
reachable(R,C) :- start(R,C).

% Zero cells that are interior (cannot reach the border)
interior_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), not reachable(R,C).

% Output rules
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0.                % keep a non‑zero colour
output(R,C,0) :- input(R,C,0), reachable(R,C).        % zeros that can reach the border stay 0
output(R,C,X) :- input(R,C,0), interior_zero(R,C), X = W + 1. % interior zeros become wall+1

% Exactly one colour per position
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% Offsets for 4‑connected neighbourhoods
delta(0,1). delta(0,-1). delta(1,0). delta(-1,0).

<asp>
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI transformation:
%  * Find the smallest colour on the outer border (the “wall”)
%    – every non‑zero cell on the border with colour ≠ wall starts a flood‑fill.
%  * The flood‑fill spreads only through cells that are NOT the wall colour.
%    * A zero cell that is reached by a flood‑fill whose source colour is V
%      becomes colour V (the source colour – 0 if the source is a border zero).
%    * Zero cells that cannot be reached become wall+1.
%  * All other cells keep their original colour.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid dimensions -------------------------------------------------
row_min(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min{R : input(R, _, _)}.
row_max(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max{R : input(R, _, _)}.
col_min(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min{C : input(_, C, _)}.
col_max(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% ----- border cells ----------------------------------------------------
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = Rmin.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = Rmax.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = Cmin.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = Cmax.

% ----- colour that appears on the border (non‑zero) --------------------
border_nonzero(Col) :- input(R,C,Col), boundary(R,C), Col != 0.

% ----- smallest border colour (the “wall”) ---------------------------
wall(W) :- border_nonzero(W), not border_nonzero(C), C < W.

% ----- start cells for the flood‑fill (border cells whose colour ≠ wall) --
start(V,R,C) :- input(R,C,V), boundary(R,C), V != W.

% ----- flood‑fill through cells that are not the wall colour ----------
% base case: the start cells themselves
reachable(V,R0,C0) :- start(V,R0,C0), input(R0,C0,X), X != W.

% propagation step
reachable(V,R2,C2) :- reachable(V,R1,C1), delta(DR,DC),
                       R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
                       input(R2,C2,X), X != W,
                       reachable(V,R1,C1).

% ----- zero cells that are reached by a flood‑fill --------------------
reachable_zero(V,R,C) :- reachable(V,R,C), input(R,C,0).

% there is at least one reachable zero for a cell
any_zero(R,C) :- reachable_zero(V,R,C).

% interior zero cells (cannot be reached from the border)
interior_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), not any_zero(R,C).

% ----- output for the three kinds of cells ---------------------------
% 1. non‑zero cells keep their original colour
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% 2. zero cells that are reachable become the colour of the flood‑fill source
output(R,C,V) :- reachable_zero(V,R,C).

% 3. interior zero cells become wall+1
output(R,C,Color) :- interior_zero(R,C), Color = W+1.

% ----- exactly one colour per position ----------------------------------
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% ----- 4‑connected neighbourhood offsets -------------------------------
delta(0,1).   delta(0,-1).   delta(1,0).   delta(-1,0).

% ----- constraints to force the correct colour -------------------------
% 1. non‑zero cells keep their original colour
:- input(R,C,V), V != 0, output(R,C,Color), Color != V.

% 2. reachable zero cells keep the colour of the flood‑fill source
:- reachable_zero(V,R,C), output(R,C,Color), Color != V.

% 3. interior zero cells become wall+1
:- interior_zero(R,C), output(R,C,Color), Color != W+1.
</asp>

<asp>
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI transformation:
%  * Find the smallest colour on the outer border (the “wall”).
%  * Flood‑fill from every border cell whose colour is NOT the wall,
%    moving only through cells whose colour ≠ wall.
%  * A zero cell that is reached by this flood‑fill stays 0.
%  * A zero cell that cannot be reached becomes wall+1.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid dimensions -------------------------------------------------
row_cnt(Rcnt) :- Rcnt = #max{R : input(R, _, _)} + 1.
col_cnt(Ccnt) :- Ccnt = #max{C : input(_, C, _)} + 1.

% ----- outer border (any cell on the outermost row or column) ----------
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = 0.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = Rcnt - 1.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = 0.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = Ccnt - 1.

% ----- border non‑zero colours ------------------------------------------
border_nonzero(V) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% ----- choose the wall colour (smallest border non‑zero colour) ---------
1 { wall(W) : color(W) } 1 :- border_nonzero(W).

% enforce that W is the smallest border colour (no smaller border colour exists)
:- wall(W), #count{V : border_nonzero(V), V < W} = 0.

% ----- flood‑fill start cells (border cells whose colour ≠ wall) ----------
reachable_any(R,C) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != W, wall(W).

% propagation: move to 4‑neighbours, staying away from wall cells ----------
reachable_any(R2,C2) :- reachable_any(R1,C1), delta(DR,DC),
                       R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
                       input(R2,C2,Col), Col != W, wall(W).

% zero cells that are reachable become 0
reachable_zero(R,C) :- reachable_any(R,C), input(R,C,0).

% zero cells that are not reachable become interior zeros
interior_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), not reachable_zero(R,C).

% ----- exactly one colour per position ------------------------------------
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% ----- 4‑connected neighbourhood offsets ---------------------------------
delta(0,1).   delta(0,-1).   delta(1,0).   delta(-1,0).

% ----- constraints to enforce the correct colour for each cell -------------
% 1. non‑zero cells keep their original colour
:- input(R,C,V), V != 0, output(R,C,Color), Color != V.

% 2. reachable zero cells stay 0
:- reachable_any(R,C), input(R,C,0), output(R,C,Color), Color != 0.

% 3. interior zero cells become wall+1
:- interior_zero(R,C), output(R,C,Color), wall(W), W != 0, Color != W+1.
</asp>

% Determine grid dimensions
row_cnt(NR) :- NR = #max{R : input(R, _, _)}.
col_cnt(NC) :- NC = #max{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% border cells: any cell on outer rows or columns

border(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = 0.

% Determine colour on border that is smallest non-zero
border_nonzero(V) :- border(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% wall is smallest colour on border (non-zero)
wall(W) :- border_nonzero(W), #count{V : border_nonzero(V), V < W} = 0.

% start cells: border cells whose colour != wall
start(R,C) :- border(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != W.

% flood fill: reachable cells (any colour) reachable from start without crossing wall
reachable(R2,C2) :- reachable(R1,C1), delta(DR,DC),
                   R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
                   input(R2,C2,X), X != W,
                   reachable(R1,C1).
reachable(R,C) :- start(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != W.

% zero cells reachable
reachable_zero(R,C) :- reachable(R,C), input(R,C,0).

% interior zero cells (not reachable)
interior_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), not reachable(R,C).

% Output definitions
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

% Choose exactly one colour for each cell
1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% 4-connected neighbours offsets
delta(0,1). delta(0,-1). delta(1,0). delta(-1,0).

% Constraints
% non-zero cells keep original colour
:- input(R,C,V), V != 0, output(R,C,Color), Color != V.

% reachable zero cells become 0
:- reachable(R,C), input(R,C,0), output(R,C,Color), Color != 0.

% interior zero cells become wall+1
:- interior_zero(R,C), wall(W), output(R,C,Color), Color != W+1.

% Offsets as above (already defined)
```

refinements.response:
```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI transformation:
%  * The smallest colour on the outer border (ignoring zeros) is the “wall”.
%  * Flood‑fill starts at every border cell whose colour is not the wall
%    and spreads only through cells whose colour is not the wall.
%  * A zero cell that is reached by the flood‑fill stays 0.
%  * A zero cell that is not reached becomes wall+1.
%  * All other cells keep their original colour.
% ------------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------- grid dimensions ------------------------------------------------
row_max(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max{R : input(R, _, _)}.
col_max(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% ---------- border cells (any cell on the outermost row or column) -------
border(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = 0.
border(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = Rmax.
border(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = 0.
border(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = Cmax.

% ---------- colour that appears on the border (non‑zero) --------------------
border_nonzero(V) :- border(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% ---------- the wall: smallest colour on the border (non‑zero) ----------
wall(W) :- border_nonzero(W), #count{V : border_nonzero(V), V < W} = 0.

% ---------- start cells for the flood‑fill (border cells whose colour ≠ wall)
start(R,C,V) :- border(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != W.

% ---------- flood‑fill: reachable cells (any colour) -------------------------
reachable(R2,C2) :- reachable(R1,C1), delta(DR,DC),
                   R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
                   input(R2,C2,X), X != W,
                   reachable(R1,C1).
reachable(R,C) :- start(R,C,V), input(R,C,V), V != W.

% ---------- zero cells reached by the flood‑fill -----------------------------
reachable_zero(R,C) :- reachable(R,C), input(R,C,0).

% ---------- interior zero cells (not reachable) ---------------------------
interior_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), not reachable(R,C).

% ---------- output handling -------------------------------------------------
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% 4‑connected neighbourhood offsets
delta(0,1).   delta(0,-1).   delta(1,0).   delta(-1,0).

% ---------- constraints ------------------------------------------------------
% 1. non‑zero cells keep their original colour
:- input(R,C,V), V != 0, output(R,C,Color), Color != V.

% 2. zero cells that are reachable stay 0
:- reachable(R,C), input(R,C,0), output(R,C,Color), Color != 0.

% 3. interior zero cells become wall+1
:- interior_zero(R,C), wall(W), output(R,C,Color), Color != W+1.
```

% ------------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI transformation:
%  * The smallest colour on the outer border (ignoring zeros) is the “wall”.
%  * Flood‑fill starts at every border cell whose colour is not the wall
%    and spreads only through cells whose colour is not the wall.
%  * A zero cell that is reached by the flood‑fill stays 0.
%  * A zero cell that is not reached becomes wall+1.
%  * All other cells keep their original colour.
% ------------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------- grid dimensions ------------------------------------------------
row_max(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max{R : input(R, _, _)}.
col_max(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% ---------- border cells (any cell on the outermost row or column) -------
border(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = 0.
border(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = Rmax.
border(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = 0.
border(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = Cmax.

% ---------- colour that appears on the border (non‑zero) --------------------
border_nonzero(V) :- border(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% ---------- the wall: smallest colour on the border (non‑zero) ----------
wall(W) :- border_nonzero(W), #count{V : border_nonzero(V), V < W} = 0.

% ---------- start cells for the flood‑fill (border cells whose colour ≠ wall)
start(R,C,V) :- border(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != W.

% ---------- flood‑fill: reachable cells (any colour) -------------------------
reachable(R2,C2) :- reachable(R1,C1), delta(DR,DC),
                   R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
                   input(R2,C2,X), X != W,
                   reachable(R1,C1).
reachable(R,C) :- start(R,C,V), input(R,C,V), V != W.

% ---------- zero cells reached by the flood‑fill -----------------------------
reachable_zero(R,C) :- reachable(R,C), input(R,C,0).

% ---------- interior zero cells (not reachable) ---------------------------
interior_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), not reachable(R,C).

% ---------- output handling -------------------------------------------------
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% 4‑connected neighbourhood offsets
delta(0,1).   delta(0,-1).   delta(1,0).   delta(-1,0).

% ---------- constraints ------------------------------------------------------
% 1. non‑zero cells keep their original colour
:- input(R,C,V), V != 0, output(R,C,Color), Color != V.

% 2. zero cells that are reachable stay 0
:- reachable(R,C), input(R,C,0), output(R,C,Color), Color != 0.

% 3. interior zero cells become wall+1
:- interior_zero(R,C), wall(W), output(R,C,Color), Color != W+1.

<transformation>
The input is treated as a tile map. The output is NxM times larger than the input (where the input is N rows x M cols). Each input cell at (BR, BC) with a non-zero value causes a copy of the full input to be placed in the corresponding NxM block of the output (at rows BR*N .. BR*N+N-1, cols BC*M .. BC*M+M-1). Each input cell with value 0 produces an all-zero block at that position.
</transformation>

<asp>
% Count distinct row/col indices — equals grid dimensions for 0-indexed inputs
n_rows(N) :- N = #count{R : input(R, _, _)}.
n_cols(N) :- N = #count{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% Output is NR*NR rows and NC*NC cols (input tiled by itself)
output_cell(R, C) :-
    input(BR, BC, _), input(DR, DC, _),
    n_rows(NR), n_cols(NC),
    R = BR*NR + DR, C = BC*NC + DC.

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% Non-zero block: the sub-cell must equal the input value at the relative position
:- input(BR, BC, V), V != 0,
   input(DR, DC, TileColor),
   n_rows(NR), n_cols(NC),
   R = BR*NR + DR, C = BC*NC + DC,
   output(R, C, ActualColor), ActualColor != TileColor.

% Zero block: every sub-cell must be 0
:- input(BR, BC, 0),
   input(DR, DC, _),
   n_rows(NR), n_cols(NC),
   R = BR*NR + DR, C = BC*NC + DC,
   output(R, C, ActualColor), ActualColor != 0.
</asp>
</example_1>

<transformation>
The output is the same size as the input. Color-3 cells are unchanged. Non-3 cells (value 0) that are enclosed by color-3 walls — i.e., cannot reach the grid boundary via 4-connected movement through non-3 cells — are filled with color 4. All other non-3 cells remain 0.
</transformation>

<asp>
% Output has the same dimensions as the input
output_cell(R, C) :- input(R, C, _).
1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% Grid boundary coordinates
min_row(R) :- R = #min{Row : input(Row, _, _)}.
max_row(R) :- R = #max{Row : input(Row, _, _)}.
min_col(C) :- C = #min{Col : input(_, Col, _)}.
max_col(C) :- C = #max{Col : input(_, Col, _)}.

% 4-connected adjacency offsets
delta(0,1). delta(0,-1). delta(1,0). delta(-1,0).

% Non-3 cells on the grid boundary are reachable from outside
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, min_row(Rmin), R = Rmin.
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, max_row(Rmax), R = Rmax.
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, min_col(Cmin), C = Cmin.
outside(R, C) :- input(R, C, V), V != 3, max_col(Cmax), C = Cmax.

% Flood-fill outward through non-3 cells
outside(R2, C2) :-
    outside(R1, C1), delta(DR, DC),
    R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
    input(R2, C2, V), V != 3.

% Color-3 cells stay 3
:- input(R, C, 3), output(R, C, Color), Color != 3.

% Outside non-3 cells stay 0
:- outside(R, C), output(R, C, Color), Color != 0.

% Interior non-3 cells (enclosed by 3s, not reachable from boundary) become 4
:- input(R, C, 0), not outside(R, C), output(R, C, Color), Color != 4.
</asp>
</example_2>

<asp>
% Determine the grid dimensions
min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min{R : input(R, _, _)}.
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max{R : input(R, _, _)}.
min_col(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min{C : input(_, C, _)}.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% Border cells (any cell on the outermost row or column)
boundary(R,C) :- R = Rmin.
boundary(R,C) :- R = Rmax.
boundary(R,C) :- C = Cmin.
boundary(R,C) :- C = Cmax.

% Non‑zero colours that appear on the border
border_nonzero(V) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% Smallest border colour (the “wall” colour)
wall(W) :- #min{W : border_nonzero(W)}.

% Flood‑fill through cells that are not the wall colour
start(R,C) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != W.
reachable(R2,C2) :- reachable(R1,C1), delta(DR,DC),
                   R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
                   input(R2,C2,V), V != W.
reachable(R,C) :- start(R,C).

% Zero cells that are interior (cannot reach the border)
interior_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), not reachable(R,C).

% Output rules
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0.                % keep a non‑zero colour
output(R,C,0) :- input(R,C,0), reachable(R,C).        % zeros that can reach the border stay 0
output(R,C,X) :- input(R,C,0), interior_zero(R,C), X = W + 1. % interior zeros become wall+1

% Exactly one colour per position
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% Offsets for 4‑connected neighbourhoods
delta(0,1). delta(0,-1). delta(1,0). delta(-1,0).
</asp>

<asp>
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI transformation:
%  * Find the smallest colour on the outer border (the “wall”)
%    – every non‑zero cell on the border with colour ≠ wall starts a flood‑fill.
%  * The flood‑fill spreads only through cells that are NOT the wall colour.
%    * A zero cell that is reached by a flood‑fill whose source colour is V
%      becomes colour V (the source colour – 0 if the source is a border zero).
%    * Zero cells that cannot be reached become wall+1.
%  * All other cells keep their original colour.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid dimensions -------------------------------------------------
row_min(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min{R : input(R, _, _)}.
row_max(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max{R : input(R, _, _)}.
col_min(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min{C : input(_, C, _)}.
col_max(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% ----- border cells ----------------------------------------------------
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = Rmin.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = Rmax.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = Cmin.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = Cmax.

% ----- colour that appears on the border (non‑zero) --------------------
border_nonzero(Col) :- input(R,C,Col), boundary(R,C), Col != 0.

% ----- smallest border colour (the “wall”) ---------------------------
wall(W) :- border_nonzero(W), not border_nonzero(C), C < W.

% ----- start cells for the flood‑fill (border cells whose colour ≠ wall) --
start(V,R,C) :- input(R,C,V), boundary(R,C), V != W.

% ----- flood‑fill through cells that are not the wall colour ----------
% base case: the start cells themselves
reachable(V,R0,C0) :- start(V,R0,C0), input(R0,C0,X), X != W.

% propagation step
reachable(V,R2,C2) :- reachable(V,R1,C1), delta(DR,DC),
                       R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
                       input(R2,C2,X), X != W,
                       reachable(V,R1,C1).

% ----- zero cells that are reached by a flood‑fill --------------------
reachable_zero(V,R,C) :- reachable(V,R,C), input(R,C,0).

% there is at least one reachable zero for a cell
any_zero(R,C) :- reachable_zero(V,R,C).

% interior zero cells (cannot be reached from the border)
interior_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), not any_zero(R,C).

% ----- output for the three kinds of cells ---------------------------
% 1. non‑zero cells keep their original colour
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% 2. zero cells that are reachable become the colour of the flood‑fill source
output(R,C,V) :- reachable_zero(V,R,C).

% 3. interior zero cells become wall+1
output(R,C,Color) :- interior_zero(R,C), Color = W+1.

% ----- exactly one colour per position ----------------------------------
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% ----- 4‑connected neighbourhood offsets -------------------------------
delta(0,1).   delta(0,-1).   delta(1,0).   delta(-1,0).

% ----- constraints to force the correct colour -------------------------
% 1. non‑zero cells keep their original colour
:- input(R,C,V), V != 0, output(R,C,Color), Color != V.

% 2. reachable zero cells keep the colour of the flood‑fill source
:- reachable_zero(V,R,C), output(R,C,Color), Color != V.

% 3. interior zero cells become wall+1
:- interior_zero(R,C), output(R,C,Color), Color != W+1.
</asp>

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI transformation:
%  * Find the smallest colour on the outer border (the “wall”).
%  * Flood‑fill from every border cell whose colour is NOT the wall,
%    moving only through cells whose colour ≠ wall.
%  * A zero cell that is reached by this flood‑fill stays 0.
%  * A zero cell that cannot be reached becomes wall+1.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid dimensions -------------------------------------------------
row_cnt(Rcnt) :- Rcnt = #max{R : input(R, _, _)} + 1.
col_cnt(Ccnt) :- Ccnt = #max{C : input(_, C, _)} + 1.

% ----- outer border (any cell on the outermost row or column) ----------
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = 0.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), R = Rcnt - 1.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = 0.
boundary(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), C = Ccnt - 1.

% ----- border non‑zero colours ------------------------------------------
border_nonzero(V) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% ----- choose the wall colour (smallest border non‑zero colour) ---------
1 { wall(W) : color(W) } 1 :- border_nonzero(W).

% enforce that W is the smallest border colour (no smaller border colour exists)
:- wall(W), #count{V : border_nonzero(V), V < W} = 0.

% ----- flood‑fill start cells (border cells whose colour ≠ wall) ----------
reachable_any(R,C) :- boundary(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != W, wall(W).

% propagation: move to 4‑neighbours, staying away from wall cells ----------
reachable_any(R2,C2) :- reachable_any(R1,C1), delta(DR,DC),
                       R2 = R1+DR, C2 = C1+DC,
                       input(R2,C2,Col), Col != W, wall(W).

% zero cells that are reachable become 0
reachable_zero(R,C) :- reachable_any(R,C), input(R,C,0).

% zero cells that are not reachable become interior zeros
interior_zero(R,C) :- input(R,C,0), not reachable_zero(R,C).

% ----- exactly one colour per position ------------------------------------
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% ----- 4‑connected neighbourhood offsets ---------------------------------
delta(0,1).   delta(0,-1).   delta(1,0).   delta(-1,0).

% ----- constraints to enforce the correct colour for each cell -------------
% 1. non‑zero cells keep their original colour
:- input(R,C,V), V != 0, output(R,C,Color), Color != V.

% 2. reachable zero cells stay 0
:- reachable_any(R,C), input(R,C,0), output(R,C,Color), Color != 0.

% 3. interior zero cells become wall+1
:- interior_zero(R,C), output(R,C,Color), wall(W), W != 0, Color != W+1.
</asp>
